what_it_does: Implements a reactive workflow scheduler that compiles a graph, then repeatedly fires nodes once every declared input port is wired and its store address resolves. It memoizes deterministic runs, skips reference-resolved node types, isolates per-node failures, writes per-port outputs with provenance, and classifies unreached nodes as pruned or stuck.
observations: run() calls _compile() on every invocation, turning the graph into ExecNodes for the requested branch.
Node readiness is decided by store.head() over each declared input port, not by an in-memory queue.
The memo signature mixes node type, version, config, and a port-to-content-hash input map so port swaps change the cache key.
Nodes marked VOLATILE bypass the memo gate and are always re-run.
Reference-resolved node types (e.g. portals) are marked processed but never computed or written by the scheduler.
A run() exception is contained: the failing node is recorded in failed{}, produces no output, and the loop continues with other ready nodes.
Multi-output nodes must return a {port: value} dict; unknown ports raise ValueError; unwritten ports prune that branch.
Every output write records a Provenance entry via store.write_provenance().
Pruned vs stuck classification uses a transitive closure over unwritten ports of multi-output nodes that ran or were memo-skipped.
pause and force are simple node-id sets that alter dispatch and memo behavior.
standouts: Store-driven readiness makes resume-across-process a natural consequence of reading the store.
The memo gate unifies re-run-only-what-changed and resume into one mechanism.
Fail-loud per-node error containment keeps one bad node from aborting the whole run.
Selective emission from multi-output nodes is used as the branching/pruning primitive.
conventions: Docstring-first with design rationale and rule references (e.g. fail-loud, rule 4).
Uses a store abstraction for all persistence, resolution, memoization, and lineage.
Type hints on public function signatures.
Mutable sets/dicts accumulate state across the resolution loop.
Raises ValueError for programmer-level contract violations (wrong return shape, unknown port).
Branch, pause, and force are treated as live addressing/dispatch controls rather than control-flow constructs.
concerns: _compile() runs on every scheduler invocation, which may be expensive for large graphs.
The outer while loop scans all exec nodes each iteration until no progress, giving O(n^2) behavior in the worst case.
The pruned/stuck closure recomputes feeds_pruned inside the inner loop on every pass.
store.exists(cached) is checked but a missing cached value simply falls through to re-run without surfacing the inconsistency.
Reference-resolved nodes produce no scheduler output, so downstream nodes depending solely on them will land in stuck/pruned unless resolved elsewhere.
notes: This file is the core resolver: it turns a compiled graph into store-address-driven node firings, with memoization and failure containment as first-class mechanics.
questions: What are the exact semantics of store.head, store.memo_get, store.exists, and store.set_ref?
How does _compile encode branch names into per-node addresses and validate node types?
How are reference-resolved node types consumed at view time (e.g. by Suite.state)?
What guarantees does store.put_content provide for idempotency and atomicity of memo content?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches — the file implements the stated store-driven reactive scheduler, memo gate, branch/pause/force addressing controls, and resolver-not-control-flow design.
apparent_themes: selective emission for branching and pruning
provenance and lineage on every write
scheduler as resolver, not control flow
intention_signals: The code is trying to make workflow execution a pure function of store state so that resuming across processes, incremental recomputation, and branching all collapse into address-level operations.
novelty: The pruned-vs-stuck classification is built from deliberately unwritten multi-output ports, and the memo gate keys on per-port content hashes so wiring order affects cache correctness.
contribution: A store-driven reactive scheduler that fires nodes when declared inputs resolve; a per-port content-hash memo gate for deterministic skip/resume; per-node failure containment that records errors without aborting the run; multi-output selective emission with transitive pruned/stuck classification; and provenance recording on every output write.
summary_for_embedding: runtime/scheduler.py implements a store-driven reactive workflow scheduler with per-port memoization, failure containment, multi-output selective emission, and pruned/stuck classification.
intention_for_embedding: To make workflow execution resumeable, incremental, and branch-aware by resolving and firing nodes purely through store addresses and memo signatures.

symbols.does: Computes a deterministic memo signature from node type, version, config, and per-port input content hashes.
symbols.description: Builds a JSON payload keyed by type, version, config, and inputs (port to content-hash), serializes it with sorted keys, and returns a blake2b hex digest prefixed with sig:. Because the input map is port-aware, swapping values across ports changes the signature, preventing mis-caching of non-commutative nodes.

symbols.does: Compiles and resolves a reactive workflow by firing nodes when store-resolved inputs are ready, applying memoization, branching, pause, force, failure containment, and pruned/stuck classification.
symbols.description: Compiles the graph into ExecNodes for the given branch, then iteratively finds nodes whose declared input ports are wired and resolved in the store. It runs or memo-skips each ready node, writes per-port outputs with provenance, and classifies remaining nodes as pruned (downstream of an unwritten multi-output port) or stuck. Returns a dictionary reporting ran, skipped, stuck, pruned, failed, held, and compiled counts/sets.
